beats.activity: Wake up, shower, coffee, check weather and work schedule
beats.reasoning: Mia is a meticulous planner and workaholic; she starts early on a sunny Monday to set the tone for her day. She's checking her spreadsheets and noting the day's conditions.
Mia is on a 24-hour firefighter rotation starting early Monday; she needs to arrive before 9am to start her shift. Light Rail closure doesn't affect her route via car across Lake Washington.

beats.activity: 24-hour firefighter shift, morning tasks and training
beats.reasoning: Mia's shift begins; she handles station duties and equipment checks. The 11am FIFA match is a distraction but not relevant to her workday at the station.
Firefighters eat at station during shifts; Mia likely meal-prepped or has a protein-focused lunch to fuel her competitive energy.

beats.activity: Afternoon and evening firefighter duties, equipment maintenance
beats.reasoning: Mia continues her 24-hour rotation through afternoon and early evening, staying focused on station responsibilities.
Firefighters share meals; Mia values close relationships with her crew and uses this time to bond over food.

beats.activity: Late shift duties, night patrols, and station downtime
beats.reasoning: Mia completes her 24-hour shift, handling evening emergencies and preparing for the overnight portion of her rotation.

beats.activity: Sleep at station during overnight shift
beats.reasoning: Firefighters sleep at the station during 24-hour rotations; Mia gets 7 hours of rest before her shift ends Tuesday morning. The sleep beat spans overnight as required.

diary:
Woke up to perfect weather—one of those rare June mornings that makes you grateful for the Pacific Northwest. Coffee tasted better than usual, maybe because I knew it'd be a long day ahead. The drive to Redmond was smooth; I heard on the radio that the World Cup match was starting downtown, bars packed already, but I was headed the opposite direction.

At the station, we drilled equipment checks and ran through scenarios. Nothing too intense this morning. Grabbed lunch with the crew around noon—someone brought homemade lasagna, which beat the usual station fare. The afternoon blurred into routine maintenance, checking hoses, restocking supplies. By dinner I was already feeling that familiar shift-fatigue creeping in. Spent the evening doing night patrols around the building, mostly quiet. The station feels different after dark—peaceful, almost. Heading to bunk soon, ready for whatever the night brings.
thought_process: I chose to focus on the small pleasures of the day—good coffee, homemade food, perfect weather—because those moments matter during long shifts. The World Cup buzz downtown didn't really touch me; I'm out here in Redmond while the city celebrates. The Light Rail closure didn't affect my commute, which I'm grateful for. My mood stayed steady throughout, the kind of professional calm you develop after decades in this job. I wasn't looking for drama; the routine itself is grounding. By evening, fatigue is natural but not unwelcome—it means I'll sleep well on the bunk, and tomorrow I'll head home ready to disconnect.
world_event_prompt: Sunny Monday in early June, high of 74F. FIFA World Cup group stage match (USA vs Wales) airs at 11am PDT — bars downtown open early. Light Rail closed between Westlake and University District 9am-3pm for maintenance.
